speaker: interviewer
text: So we stopped where you were in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my parents were in peril. You know, they, they came all the time over, asking where we went. They couldn't find us. So my parents couldn't be hiding by themselves. And they-- you know, the hiding place had to be very quiet. Nobody should know that you're there. And the people are-- the people who hide you are the same persecuted as the people who were-- those people were very brave. It was a big, big thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Big risk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very big risk. So he said right away, take your parents too. So we are four there. They were very, very nice to us. So good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in that time, Germany took all the food out of Holland, the most. And the people get all of the things in-- like in, in-- in little, how you say it, stickers? Like, you had the bread was in a bond. How you call it? Bread lines?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you could buy bread here. Like the card--

speaker: interviewer
text: Food stamps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Food stamps. We got food stamps. And it was for milk, for butter, for everything that you want to buy. So that for people who-- people in hiding have it very hard. And they get two breads instead of five or four, You couldn't buy what you want. That time, the underground in Holland start already to work. It was a big underground. They were sabotaging the airways, and, you know, shoot people. And they take-- they really-- they really try to, to, to undermine the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a big underground, and they also made false food cards. So that they knew already about a grapevine of people who wanted food cards, people who helped other people in underground. And so, so the people who made the cards knew that four people are hiding by Mr. Jansen. Jan Jansen was his name. Like Martin. Martin was actually his name. I knew Marty-- Martin. And they-- so they got the cards from the-- from the underground. Every month, they get so many cards. Not only me. It was-- we got cards. My brother, who was with my sister who were hiding somewhere, they got cards. My other sister got cards. Everybody. Not only our family, but they had a whole bunch of people. So they suspected that one of the people worked in underground. And they got him. And they nearly killed him. And he had on him addresses, and our address was on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So middle in the night, the SS came around to place where we lived, came around us. And they just-- the guy where we were in hiding, like John, John, who climbed over a wall. But she was actually German there-- like Mimi, you know? So she came in. And the police said, where are they? They came right away upstairs, and they find us. We had a hiding place. We had no time to go in. So we were there, my husband, like [INAUDIBLE], and then my parents and me. And they came. They took us out of the house. And-- Do you know what they did to your landlords, the people that were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they did-- he got away.

speaker: interviewer
text: They got away?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have to tell you a little story about him. We got away. There was [INAUDIBLE]. He lived by his mother for a while. And then he took other four Jewish people in after us. And he got them out of the-- and end of the war, he saved those people. So he was really somebody special. Really one. And so we-- so, now I have to go back. When we got picked up, they took us to a police station in Haarlem. Haarlem. And there was a police station--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's Haarlem?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the suburb from Heemstede, and this, this a decent town. It's not that small town. It is quite-- it's a town. And they-- so we were there, maybe for an hour. And then my brother and my sister-in-law came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had rounded them up as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also from the-- from the the--

speaker: interviewer
text: From the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who else?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my, my oldest sister, who went away. But she had a baby. And then the SS came in the hiding place. She went back for the baby, and they took her with the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: They killed her?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed her. Both.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the spot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They took them also to-- I don't know. They took them to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw her again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never saw her again. I know that when we were in the police station, my sister-in-law and my brother came in. And they were crying so much. And I said-- he said, you don't know what happened to us. There was a little boy hiding also, Jewish boy. He was six years, five years old, living also in their place. And the landlord [INAUDIBLE], he did not want to give the kid to the SSers. And they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: They shot the landlord?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just where the parents and everybody was there. So my brother-in-law, my sister, was absolutely out of their mind. And they took the kid anyway, never saw him back. So they, they were crying. And you know, what happened to them, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other Jews at this police station at the same time with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were-- in Haarlem, the only one. We were-- two, four, six-- six of us. They took was in the night, took us in a big truck. And they took us to Amsterdam. Amsterdam, there was a big theater. And all the people from the [NON-ENGLISH] and from the people who were picked up like us. And it was quite a big-- the big theater. was full. Maybe 200, 300 people were laying on the-- on the floor on mattresses, and blankets, and things like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was the Netherlands Schouwburg. It was-- it was a theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: A movie--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: A stage theater or a movie theater? What type of a theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a stage theater. And they made it empty, and they put Jewish people in-- Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. And it is still there in Amsterdam. They made a museum out of it in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, the people, was like-- they, they put them all together there. And the SS came in and out, and you know, and there was a lot of-- at that time, when we came in the theater, it was all very bad. I saw people commit suicide. And, and-- Was there any food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they gave food. They gave enough food. And it was still-- the Dutch people were still a little bit-- Dutch community, they made sure there was food. There was blankets. There was drink, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But you see, when we came in Schouwburg, they knew we were picked up from the-- from the-- out of hiding place. And they say, a-ha. You came-- you were a year and a half in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave us with paint, red paint, big, big S on our clothes. S. S was for staff, for punishment. So the punishment was we should go the first, when there was transports to go. You should be the first transport out.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. We should go the first on transport. So we were there only one or two nights. And the transport-- and then we sent us to Westerbork where the trains leaves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in, in tractors, like closed, and with police and SS, you know? So then we went to Westerbork. And we-- the men were separate, and the women were separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were on this tractor to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. Maybe 40, 50. I don't-- [CLEARS THROAT] I can't. Really I can't. I, I-- so then we-- it was a lot. But it was, you know, just not that far. It was maybe two hours or-- so we were there. And then we get food in Westerbork. And the men and the women were separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: I sneaked-- instead of staying with the women, I went with the men. And I said to my husband, I stay with you the night, you know? He said, but it is dangerous. There was more people around him. He said, if you're quiet, and then you could stay together. So in the middle of the night, it was the big tumult. And they looked for people they wanted to take. So my husband was afraid they would find me in-- with him. So they climbed out of his bed. And they, they took him and other people out of Westerbork. And in that night, there were 12 people shot. They were [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- the Germans killed them for they were in the underground. And they were like-- they were against the Germans. And they were not Jewish. They were all gentile. And my husband was artist, and was actually not used to, to killings and things. He had to bury them. He had to take-- they were blindfolded. They were shot. And he had too--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he had to bury them in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had to bury them. He had to take them off the pole. And he was all, when he came back-- I didn't see him before nearly the train left. I was there. I was frantic that he went to be-- and we didn't know where he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some, some people put me back to the women's, some boys that I knew already, Dutch people, said, you go back to, to your mother. And then I know he was picked up, but I didn't know for what. So then the next morning, our names were right away called up. SS came with the names-- my parents, my sister-in-law, my brother, my mother, my father. And when we came to the trains, and who comes on? Max, my-- like my first husband, and crying. You know, he said, I just buried people. I am absolutely crazy, you know? So, so we went-- so-- and the trains were just cattle wagons. There was a little opening in the top. There was a bucket of water and a bucket for-- to go to the washroom. And there was some food in it. You could-- I don't know. There was some food in it. And-- Did you say there was food in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was food. Yeah, there was, like, cucumbers and bread. I don't know what was in it. I didn't eat. I remember that. So we were in there. And I-- when I came close to the, to the train, I saw a [INAUDIBLE] it said Auschwitz. I heard a little bit about Auschwitz, but I didn't know what. So we--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memory how long you were on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's at least five, six days.

speaker: interviewer
text: At least five, six days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Five, six--

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people were on that train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that is a-- you nearly couldn't lay down. You had to sit up nearly, it was so full on the-- one bucket with all that people. Can you imagine how it stunk? But after, you know, it was-- you know, people-- you couldn't-- you couldn't go to the restroom. It was flowed over in maybe an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were sitting with my parents. It is dark. There's only a little opening, in the-- in the top. And we used to talk to each other and our hands. And we hoped that we could stay together. What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The main thing we talked about with my parents and my husband, that we should stay together. We were not afraid to work, we're workers all our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you thought you were going to do, to go to a work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I thought we could stay together, an we could work hard. And we-- and you know, and-- you know. We were hoping. We weren't sure, you know? My husband said, that that is in the pipe dream, he used to say that to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He thought it was something--

speaker: interviewee
text: He thought it would be worse than-- you know, he was more-- but we were hoping. I, I-- even if we didn't believe. We, we would-- we thought it would be very, very bad, you know? But when we-- I-- when we were in a train and after the whistles blow, and we came in the big platform. And the big lights came on, on us--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the first thing you saw when you-- when the doors opened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came out in Auschwitz, when I stepped out in the train, everybody had to get out and I stay in the train. And I said, that is a lost cause. I saw people walking around just with the striped-- you know, and the little caps, and the ears. And, and it was in September the 23rd that came in in Auschwitz. And I-- and, and I ask somebody very quietly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any smoke? Did you smell anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I smelled very rubbery smell. And you assume that Auschwitz stinks. So this smell, rubber smell, and shootings.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard shootings?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came out of the train, there were kids were crying. They were shot. And, and the men didn't like the women-- first of all, there was-- there was selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came out of the train, there was big truck staying on both sides and SS's. And one side there was Mengele, and he pushes the men all this way, and the young men there, and the women there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So right away, the people get separated. So the men didn't want the women go. The women didn't let them. There was a real chaos. What happened? Were you separated right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crying. Yeah. My, my father, and my husband, and my brother get to one side. And my mother, and my sister-in-law and me were on the other side. And, suddenly, I saw the big, big tractor stay. And young people, young women with children, and kids under 15 years old, and people older than 50. They ask you what your age were. They were thrown on the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And before I know it, they took my mother up, and they just threw her in the truck, you know? And I said, hey. I said, that's my mother, you know? And, and I know you couldn't hear. I think, what going to happen to her? Was she trying to signal you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she, she, she telled me on her hands, that truck is no good. She smelled maybe, I don't know. So I went to Mengele. And everybody was afraid. I went to him. And I said, please. I said, my mother just go on the truck and she can't hear. Or you have to let me go with her, or she has to come with me. Or let her come with me. And he looked me over. And he said, no. He said, your mother can stay easily on the truck. You have to walk. You're young. You're a strong woman. You can walk. And it is far. He said, you'll see your mother later, and you know, and let her go. My mother stays in the truck. I see the truck driving away, slowly with my mother telling me that the truck is no good, on her fingers. And I staying there and couldn't get her off. So that was unbelievable. My father was on the other side with my brother. I couldn't see him anymore. They was most probably go away right away too. So my brother and my, my husband ride together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed in the line by-- in a big line. And suddenly, Mengele comes out, and put, with his little stick, took me out, took my sister-in-law out, and about 40 people. He said, those 40 people come with the SS, and the two SS. And we had to go somewhere. So they took us in Auschwitz. Now, there was two [NON-ENGLISH]. There was one, Birkenau. The women's [NON-ENGLISH] was Auschwitz. I went to Auschwitz, where the-- where the men were. So they took us there, and there was a little [NON-ENGLISH], a little thing. And they took us away. And they took our-- I had a wedding band, and I don't-- nothing. And they took us off and out. And you had to take all your clothes off. They took away all your jewelery, whatever you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- yeah. The most was already in-- yeah, jewelry. I had a watch and a ring, and nothing special. We were not rich people. We didn't have a lot of jewelry. But we had to take it out. What they did to me, I had a gold tooth, here. One gold tooth instead of a-- and they took it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: They pulled that right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. They pulled it out, just like this. So they-- I was crying. They shaved my hair. They shaved all your body, all your things. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have your sister with you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-in-law was always with me. So she had very-- she was very, very blonde. You can see later the pictures. A beautiful woman. Very blonde, long hair, and they shaved it off. And she felt all the curls falling down. She felt terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- and I was-- that day, I had my period, you know? And no clothes, everything-- ugh. Unbelievable. You-- you-- it does not-- you can-- you can say-- The lack of dignity.

speaker: interviewee
text: The lack of decency, nothing, you know? You say [INAUDIBLE]. And you're naked, absolutely naked, before them. And then your hair cut off. And they took my tooth out. And I was so frustrated. And then the guy came who make the numbers. And he saw the blood coming out of my mouth. And he said to me, very softly in German, he said, I have to give you your number. He was also a prisoner. I'm going to make it very small. And he did. He wanted to do something for me. He didn't know what. So he gave me the smallest number who ever existed, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to just show the number to the camera? I think it's 63175.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the other ones had numbers around their whole arm. He made me-- mine very small. So and then they took us out of-- and I was still without clothes. So we went through the main [NON-ENGLISH] with all the men looking at us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Naked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had no clothes. And they took us to block 10. And there we got clothes. I Had then underwear, and a dress, and you know, something. So when I came in Block 10, and the first thing what I said, where's my mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I said where is my mother?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you said where is your mother. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know they go straight to the gas chamber then. I still had a hope that she--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were looking for your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe she would be there. And then they told me right away. She went in a truck, forget it. They go straight to the gas chamber, and they get burned. And then you're-- you're smelling Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other prisoners that had been there longer tell you what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Most probably--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they educate you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me, do you see the smoke, and the ash, you know? They get right away burned. First of all, I think that they, they say something to make me afraid, you know? But then I understand it was true.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other prisoners were receptive to the newcomers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they-- well, they are-- first of all, don't forget I-- when we were there for an hour or two, they came-- the SS came with a-- with a big paper. And they said to, to us, I have to say something to you. Here's an experiment block. They do experiments on human beings. But you don't have to. You have to sign it. You do it out of free will. If you don't want to sign, you go tomorrow morning. The truck comes, and you go to the gas chamber. Or you sign and you let the experiments do it to-- you know, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, we were three-- when we were in hiding until 1943. And we heard already that the Germans were not so-- they lost already quite a bit battles. And we said to each other, my sister-in-law, let us try. Maybe we can stay alive. Maybe the war is soon over. So you signed the papers, and you became part of the experiment block.

speaker: interviewee
text: We signed the papers. We did sign the papers that we have to-- yeah. So middle in the night, I hear screaming and crying. And people with big holes in their body, and then and that. And I say to my sister-in-law, I not going to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that the experiments doing what they do to you, you know? I said, I'm not going to stay here, you know? I said, I'd rather die right away. People with, you know, no leg, You know, it was awful. It was like a nightmare. So I said to my sister-in-law, I, I not going to do that. We tell them tomorrow morning, we want to go. So at least we gassed, but it is not-- you're going to die anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said after three months, the people around as, they told, after three months, they can't use you anymore. You go anyway. They take the trucks, and they take you away. They don't let you live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the morning, we get up. We said to the people, now let us-- I say to the, to the guy there. I said, no, we'd like to go with the other ones. He said, no. You signed, and you have to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were frightened like anything, you know? I was so afraid. Unbelievable. And I said to the lady-- and the other said, no they don't always do-- there's some, some experiments are not that bad, you know? And try, you know, and some doctors try to-- not that hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Try-- they try to, to-- the people are-- there are more prisoners there that they try to-- and a lot of people did not do it and meant. So we were staying there. And then we find out after a while that the most important thing was to sterilize us. We get other-- a lot of other experiments too, but the most was to sterilize us. That's the--

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you just tell us what they did to you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they, they tried to give big injections in your womb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take you somewhere else to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was downstairs where there wasn't like in a-- in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what do you mean downstairs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Block 10 had three, three, three, three stories. Downstairs was the underside was like a laboratory. It was a big, big-- black, like a [INAUDIBLE], like an [INAUDIBLE]. And they could make like an x-ray table. They put a needle in you, and they could see that-- you could follow the-- that fluid, what they put in you, where it came. And, you know, like, they made pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did this in a building?

speaker: interviewee
text: They did it downstairs in the building.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those needles were very painful. I think they, they put some burning stuff in it. I don't know what. They was blisters and bleeding. We were screaming pretty good. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they use any anesthetic when they did this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no anesthetic. We also had to-- they, they put little pieces out of the womb.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took biopsies?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took pieces out of-- yeah. Pieces out of the womb and they make-- to make it shorter, so you could not expand and could not get children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many times did they do this to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The, the one they did only-- the operator did only one, but the needles, I had three. But I have to tell a little story. Mostly when you go in Auschwitz, you get so shocked, you don't-- your period disappeared. After the one time I came, I never had my period again. But my sister-in-law always did. Every month, she was-- and if I was-- you know, they could not work on you, the operations, if you had your period. The doctors find it most probably too messy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I was called, I took-- and you know, there was no clean clothes. I took her panty on, and went to the doctor. And said, look, I have my period. I can't do it. Did that work?

speaker: interviewee
text: That worked. Two, three times, it worked. So I was really saved from the big, big operations. I, I had the needles. I had all the other. They, they put, like, injections in one breast. And it was like this. And a week later, they gave another one. They one time-- they gave all kinds of trying experiments.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they treat us, we don't know. They never going to tell you [INAUDIBLE]. They're going to tell you [INAUDIBLE]. They don't. They took you. They did it, you know? Did you see any other women? Did you see any other experiments, besides the one that you had? Can you just tell us what else you saw there personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I know they-- no. I think that the main thing was the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: The sterilization.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also how you-- we nearly always get the same one, the injections you get in your back, and your breast, in your stomach. That was for--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Maybe they put something in the-- the sicknesses in you. Like when I-- I have-- lately, I have four, five different kinds of rheumatism. You know, that I had Dr. Bell, a specialist, said to me, I never saw a person that have four different kind of four absolutely different kind of rheumatism in one person. We know one, but four? And Dr. Bell, and she said, I don't know. I said maybe they, they do-- they did something, they put something in you. So Irving spoke to somebody from the-- from the Dutch government, and they wrote a letter back from Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did they do these experiments? How many months were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when you had-- when you had one injection, and you was bleeding, and it was all open and there was blisters, they couldn't work on you. You had to heal up first. So between, it was maybe two, three-- two months. And then there were more people. And sometimes they, they try to-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: And that whole time you were in this particular block. And all the other women in this block were also having the experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: All-- yeah. A lot of them died. You know-- and, and, you know what also, not only that you didn't get enough-- all those things, you didn't get enough to eat either. You were always hungry. OK, we know that later in that time-- we knew that the-- that, that gas chambers were our showers. And one time, the doctors were complaining to the-- to the-- to the block that the women were smelling. And  he said, I want to have to give them a bath.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine what a-- what a panic it was to give us a bath? And we had to go to a shower. We cry. We didn't know what to do. Not for sure they going to kill us, you know? Um.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every day, I was afraid to, to die. It was-- it never let off. You always-- you don't go today. So many people died. I saw so many people dead. We were in Block 10, actually in prison. It was not-- some people in the block who could walk out. But nobody's supposed to know what happened in Block 10. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- Did you have selections as well in Block 10? Was there the morning, the roll calls, the cell appel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, for hours, and hours, and hours. You were standing up. They had to tell everybody. And you saw people, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the women who were-- who were too sick to get up for cell appel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got counted separately. If you lay in bed, and they said, three people are in, in infirmary, or how do you call it? You know, they did. They said [INAUDIBLE]. Others, every day was something. There was [INAUDIBLE] people [INAUDIBLE] picking up people.
